title: The Mock Draft
summary: Run a full rookie draft against the AI in about three minutes. Pick from your queue, swap in any source you trust — Sleeper, KTC, MFL ADP — and rehearse your strategy before draft day actually matters.
description: Mock drafts in dynasty leagues used to mean either staring at FantasyPros' anonymized rookie pool with eleven strangers who'd disappear by round two, or convincing a group chat of league-mates to all hop on Discord at 9 PM on a Tuesday. The first option is a draft against bots that can't tell Tetairoa McMillan from Drake Maye. The second option is herding cats. Either way you'd burn an hour, get one bad result, and still have no idea what your board actually looks like under pressure.

The Mock Draft lobby lives at /theleague/mock-draft. Pick a timer (1, 3, 10, or 15 seconds — you're not here to think, you're here to stress-test), a round count, and how the AI picks. The board source defaults to your personal Queue from the live draft room, so the AI is genuinely drafting against your rankings instead of some generic ADP soup. You can override per team — give Pigskins your rank, give Cowboy Up the Sleeper board, give Mariachi Ninjas Chaos mode if you want to see what happens when somebody reaches for a punter in the second round. Three presets — All MFL, Mixed boards, Chaos mode — set every team in one click. Your own franchise is highlighted with a YOU badge so the seventeen-row table doesn't blur together. Hit Start Mock Draft and you're in.

Inside the room, only your slot uses the timer. Every other team picks instantly — there's no waiting on an AI to 'think.' If the AI grabs the player you wanted, hit Go Back and the slot reverts; you can rewind as far as you need to and replay from there. Pause if you need to grab a beer, Reset if you want to start fresh with the same settings. The Schefter Report doesn't see any of this — mock picks stay in your sandbox. Old sessions you don't need anymore? Hover any card in Active Sessions and a delete button appears. The rookie draft is coming up soon. This is the rehearsal.
imageAlt: The Mock Draft lobby — Start a Mock Draft form with timer chips (1, 3, 10, 15 sec), round selector, draft order toggle, and a Board Settings card showing the per-team ranking source list. The Pigskins row is highlighted with a YOU badge and defaults to My Rank; every other team is set to Default.

title: Tip Schefter Gets Louder
summary: The anonymous tip page is now a living surface — tipster scorecards, whisper-back threads, rumor reactions, hot topics, a live cooker timeline, and Tip of the Week badges. Still anonymous. Louder.
description: The original Tip Schefter page was a fire-and-forget form. You'd drop a hunch into the void, Schefter would marinate it, and at some point a rumor would show up in the feed that may or may not have been yours. No feedback, no social proof, no reason to come back. It worked, but it sat there. The Rumor Mill was getting tips — one or two a week, from the same three owners — and the rest of the league had no idea the button even existed. Time to turn the lights on without compromising anonymity.

The tip page now has a personality. Submit and you'll see your daily cap chip tick down — 2 of 3 left today — and a Last Rumor Dropped card in the sidebar shows the newest thing Schefter printed with a link into the feed. The textarea placeholder rotates by topic: trade interest, roster gripe, bold prediction, beef (covering both league-wide grievances and the commish), and other. A collapsible Good Tip vs. What Schefter Prints card shows exactly how the fuzzing works with three worked examples so nobody's guessing whether their tip is going to leak their franchise. Rumor cards in the feed now have a four-verdict reaction row — 🔥 smoke, 💯 confirmed, 🤔 fake news, 📉 cooked — that stores reactions under the one-way tipster hash instead of a franchise id, so nobody can tell who reacted what. The admin rate-limit exemption is gone too: every user is capped at 3 tips per 24 hours, full stop, because any counter surface would've otherwise leaked who the commissioners are.

The real hook is the scorecard. Every tipster that actually produces a rumor gets a stable codename — Burner Phone, Back-Channel, Smoke Signal, Off the Record, The Whisper — that's unique per owner (no #N suffixes) and stable across sessions. Your Record shows your codename, your season count, your lifetime count, and any Tip of the Week badges you've earned. Top Tipsters This Season is a codename-only leaderboard: you'll see Hearsay at 7 rumors, Cold Case at 4, and you (hopefully) at the top. Nobody knows who Hearsay is except Hearsay. Whisper Back opens a mini tip form on any rumor card so you can add context to an existing thread — the scanner groups whisper-backs, Schefter opens the next post with 'Following up on yesterday's chatter…', and the thread has its own permalink page that renders the whole chain chronologically. Add a live cooker timeline ('Schefter is marinating 2 tips. Next rumor drops in 47m.'), a Hot Topics this Week chip row showing which topics are buzzing league-wide, and a weekly Tip of the Week ritual where the most-read rumor earns badges for the tipsters that seeded it, and the page stops being a drop-off. It becomes a hangout. Anonymously, of course.
imageAlt: The Tip Schefter page with the new tipster scorecard, top-tipsters leaderboard, live cooker timeline, hot topics chips, and a rumor card in the feed showing the four-verdict anonymous reaction bar

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Mar 25-25)
summary: 4 bug fixes and style improvements across other.
description: This week's 4 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:

Other — Comment image uploads now work — images were being dropped between the composer and the API, so uploads appeared to succeed but images never showed on the posted comment. Team icons on The Board no longer have a blue circle background — they now match the transparent style used on Ask Roger. Image uploads no longer fail with a 413 error on large photos — images are now auto-compressed client-side before uploading via Vercel Blob's direct upload. Category icons on The Board now use SVG sprite icons (beer, gavel, wrench) instead of plain emoji text.

title: Set Lineup
summary: Set your starting lineup without leaving the site — tap to swap, shake to undo, and let the optimizer do the thinking.
description: Setting lineups on MFL used to mean navigating to the MFL site, logging in again because your session expired (it always expires), clicking through three menus to find the lineup page, and then squinting at a list of player IDs with all the visual polish of a 2003 HTML form. If you wanted to check matchups, injury news, or your opponent's defense ranking, that was a separate browser tab. Or three. Coach Mode on the roster page helped with the research, but actually submitting the lineup still required the MFL pilgrimage.

The Set Lineup page brings lineup management home. Nine starter slots — QB, RB, WR, TE, three FLEX, PK, DEF — each showing projection, opponent, and FPA rank at a glance. Tap any slot to open the player drawer with your full bench, sorted by projection and decorated with coach data: spread, over/under, weather, recent scoring trends, and hot streak flames for anyone on a three-week heater. Swap a player in, see the slot flash green, and your change count updates live. The Set Optimal button auto-fills the best lineup from projections (or your custom rankings if you've set them). Made a mistake? Shake your phone to undo, or tap the undo toast on desktop. When you're ready, hit Submit and it posts directly to MFL — no tab-switching, no re-authentication, no decoding player IDs.
It's mobile-first because nobody's setting lineups from a desktop at 12:58 PM on Sunday. But if you are, it works there too.
imageAlt: The Set Lineup page showing nine starter slots with player photos, projections, and opponent matchup data, with the player selection drawer open on the right

title: The 2026 Auction Recap
summary: Claude Schefter grades every franchise's auction haul — from the Pigskins' $32.75M spending spree to Dead Cap Walking's bargain bin strategy.
description: The offseason auction wrapped and Claude Schefter immediately locked himself in the film room with a spreadsheet and a strong opinion. The result is a full-length feature article breaking down every franchise's auction strategy — who spent wisely, who overpaid, and who let Lamar Jackson slip through their fingers at $5M while fighting over wide receivers at $10M. If you participated in the auction and want to know whether your front office decisions were brilliant or reckless, Schefter has thoughts. He always has thoughts.

The recap covers the Big Spenders (Pacific Pigskins, $32.75M across twelve players), the Best Values (Lamar at $5M, Kyler at $4M, Barkley at $5M), the Overpays (Justin Jefferson at $9.8M, CeeDee at $9.5M, Amon-Ra at $10M), position spending trends, and franchise-by-franchise grades from A to C+. It's the kind of article you read once to see how your team did, and then read again to laugh at what your rivals spent. This is the first feature article from The Schefter Report — expect more throughout the season.
Money is nice, but championships are better. Schefter said that, and he's probably right.
imageAlt: The Schefter Report auction recap article showing Claude Schefter's analysis of the 2026 auction results

title: The Schefter Report
summary: Claude Schefter is on the beat — a league insider who tracks every trade, auction win, and waiver claim so you don't have to.
description: Keeping up with league transactions used to require checking the MFL transaction log, which has all the personality of a database dump and the readability of a stock ticker from 1987. You'd see '0009 traded 14358,0524,FP_0009_2026_3 to 0011 for 14789,0525' and have to decode it like a war-time cipher just to find out who got fleeced. Half the league wouldn't notice a blockbuster trade until two weeks later when the new player showed up in someone's starting lineup.

The Schefter Report is a Bluesky-style social feed covering every transaction in the league. Claude Schefter — the league's AI beat reporter — breaks trades with full analysis and letter grades, reports auction wins with salary context, and logs waiver claims. Breaking-tier posts (trades, big-money auctions) get the full insider treatment: 'I'm told the Pacific Pigskins have agreed to a blockbuster deal...' Standard moves get a quick, punchy report. Minor claims get grouped into collapsible sections so they don't crowd the real news. The feed lives at /theleague/news with filter pills for Trades, Free Agency, and Auction — plus a widget on the homepage showing the last five posts.

Schefter scans for new transactions every hour, so the feed stays current without anyone lifting a finger. He's opinionated, he grades trades, and he's never wrong — or at least he's never admitted it. Think of him as your league's beat writer who actually reads the transaction log so you don't have to. Boom.
imageAlt: The Schefter Report feed page showing Claude Schefter's persona header with avatar and bio, followed by a social feed of auction wins and trade reports with filter pills for All, Trades, Free Agency, and Auction

title: The Board
summary: The league finally leaves the 2000s. A modern discussion board replaces The League message board — no more phpBB3, no more forgotten passwords, and no more posting contracts only to find out the board's been down for a week.
description: Let's talk about The League message board. The one powered by phpBB3 — the forum software that peaked the same year as flip phones and has aged about as gracefully. And the login experience — genuinely a gauntlet. You'd try to log in, realize you forgot your password, request a reset, then discover you couldn't remember your username either. So you'd message the Commissioner, who'd have to dig through the phpBB admin panel — a UI designed by people who actively resented their users — just to tell you your new password is 'pigskins_rule_07.' An entire afternoon, gone.

The Board is a full discussion platform built directly into the site — no external forum, no separate login, no phpBB admin panel, no password you set in 2009 and haven't thought about since. Post an idea, tag it as a rule change, website suggestion, or general topic, and other owners can comment, react with emoji, and actually engage in the same decade the conversation started. Each post gets its own thread with nested replies, image uploads, and an emoji reaction bar. The Commissioner can set statuses — Under Review, Approved, Rejected, Tabled — so proposals don't disappear into the void like they did when they were buried under three pages of off-topic replies about someone's fantasy football podcast. Website suggestions get a structured form that captures the page, the problem, and the desired behavior — and here's where it gets interesting: an army of AI agents is standing by to actually build the thing. Your suggestion isn't just a note in a backlog. It's a work order. Describe what you want, and the dev team — which at this point is mostly Claude wearing different hats — gets to work.

Gone are the days of posting your contract declarations to the message board, only to realize a week later that the board had quietly stopped working and nobody noticed. Rule changes still require a 75% vote per the constitution — The Board doesn't change that. But now the discussion, the reactions, and the Commissioner's ruling all live in one place instead of scattered across a dead phpBB3 instance, a group chat, and someone's DMs. Pour one out for The League message board. It survived the Obama administration, two league expansions, and an era where Comic Sans and Times New Roman coexisted on the same page. It earned its retirement.
imageAlt: The Board page showing league discussion posts with emoji reactions and comment counts, category filter pills, and a sidebar with posting guidelines and an Ask Roger promo

title: Ask Roger
summary: An AI rules expert who's read the constitution cover to cover — so you don't have to argue about franchise tag compensation at 11 PM.
description: Every dynasty league eventually develops its own oral tradition — half-remembered rules about extension windows, conflicting interpretations of compensatory pick eligibility, and that one guy who swears the taxi squad salary thing works differently than it actually does. Until now, settling these disputes required either reading all 27 sections of the constitution or pinging the Commissioner, who was probably asleep, busy, or both.

Ask Roger is an AI-powered rules Q&A board where you can type a question in plain English and get an answer grounded in the actual league constitution. Roger has read every section, every footnote, and the fine print about compensatory picks that even the Commissioner forgot about. He'll tell you the franchise tag costs your 1st and 2nd round picks, that practice squad players count at 50% salary, and that the playoff tiebreaker goes H2H → Division Record → All-Play before anyone reaches for a coin. The page ships with 32 pre-seeded Q&As covering the most common and most arcane rules, and every new question becomes part of the living archive. If Roger doesn't know something, he'll say so — which is more than you can say for most rules lawyers.

Roger's answers are *probably* right, but he's a chatbot, not the Commissioner. For definitive rulings, ask the real one. For everything else — salary escalation math at midnight, extension eligibility windows, whether you can start a player on bye — Roger's got you. Each question costs about a dime to answer, so if he saved you a trip to the rulebook, consider giving him a hug. Or PayPal. Either works.
imageAlt: Ask Roger page showing a two-column layout with AI-answered rules Q&A cards on the left and Roger's personality card on the right

title: Projected Free Agents
summary: A report showing every player in their final contract year — the ones who'll hit free agency after the season ends, ranked by projection with salary and stats.
description: Planning for free agency used to mean scrolling through sixteen roster pages, mentally flagging every player with a '1' in the contract years column, and trying to remember which ones were actually good. If you wanted salary context or projections alongside that list, you were building your own spreadsheet — and by the time you finished, someone else had already made the trade you were planning.

The Projected Free Agents report pulls every rostered player with one year remaining on their contract into a single table, sorted by projected points. You get their salary, age, last season's stats, and PPG — plus your imported dynasty rankings if you've got them. Filter by position, search by name, or narrow it down to a specific team's expiring players. The summary bar at the top shows the total count, total salary on the chopping block, and average age of the class.

Think of it as your offseason scouting report for the players who are about to become available — whether you're planning to re-sign your own guys, target someone else's expiring asset in a trade, or just want to know which teams are about to lose half their roster.
imageAlt: Projected Free Agents report showing a sortable table of players in their final contract year with salary, projected points, and position filter pills

title: Search
summary: A searchable index of every page on the site — because even the person who built it can't always remember where everything is.
description: Finding a page on this site used to require one of three things: knowing exactly where it was in the navigation drawer, remembering the URL, or asking the commissioner like he's some kind of fantasy football concierge. The site has over 30 pages across rosters, contracts, reports, and tools — and if you didn't already know the League Summary existed, you weren't going to stumble into it by accident.

Search is a searchable, categorized index of every page on the site. Type 'money' and you'll get Salary Analytics, Roster/Salary, and the Salary Archive — ranked by which pages people actually visit most. Type 'bracket' and Playoffs surfaces immediately. Filter by category chips — Popular, My Team, Reports, Tools, Info — or just browse the grid. Each card shows the page title, a short description, and the top search tags, so you can find what you need even if you don't know what it's called.

Every new page that gets added to the site automatically gets a Search entry with its own set of search keywords. Think of it as the site's table of contents, except it actually updates itself and doesn't require you to scroll past 200 pages of appendices to find the one thing you wanted.
imageAlt: Search page showing a searchable grid of page cards with category filter chips and search input

title: Owner Activity Report
summary: See exactly when each franchise owner last visited the site — ranked from most to least active, with color-coded status indicators.
description: For years, figuring out whether a trade partner was actually alive required the fantasy football equivalent of a wellness check — send a message, wait three days, check if they'd made a waiver claim this decade, then resign yourself to the possibility that your blockbuster offer was sitting in an inbox nobody had opened since the Falcons blew a 28-3 lead.

The Owner Activity page ranks all 16 franchises by their last visit to theleague.us, complete with green/yellow/red status dots so you can tell at a glance who's been grinding the waiver wire at 2 AM and who apparently thinks the offseason is a time for 'going outside.' Each team's roster header now shows a subtle last-seen timestamp too — because knowing whether someone will actually respond to your trade offer before the auction starts is, technically, useful information.

Built on the same real-time tracking that'll power future features like trade builder activity badges and commissioner engagement alerts. Think of it as a league-wide pulse monitor — except instead of a heartbeat, it's measuring how recently someone refreshed their salary cap page at work.
imageAlt: Owner Activity page showing a 30-day line chart of daily page views per owner with 16 color-coded team lines, legend, and popular pages section

title: Live Auction on Free Agents
summary: The Free Agent page now has a dedicated Auction view with live bid tracking — see every active bid, who's winning, current prices, and how much time is left, all updated every 60 seconds.
description: Tracking a live auction used to mean camping on MFL's auction page in a second tab, refreshing every few minutes, and hoping you didn't miss the 36-hour deadline on that sleeper WR you'd been eyeing since the RSP dropped. The new Auction view puts the entire bid board right next to the player data you already use.

Switch to Auction view and you'll see Current Bid, High Bidder, Time Left with a live countdown, and a Place Bid button for every nominated player. The table auto-sorts by Time Left so expiring bids surface to the top. Urgency tiers kick in as deadlines approach — amber when closing, red with a warning icon under six hours, and a pulsing alert in the final hour. Countdowns tick every 15 seconds normally and accelerate to 5-second updates when things get critical.

The Value tab also picked up live bid data — a Bid column right next to your estimated cost and surplus value analysis shows exactly what each player sold for. Won auctions appear in bold so you can spot completed sales at a glance. Your My Rank composite and average rank now appear alongside the money columns, so you can cross-reference your board with market prices without switching views.

Data refreshes automatically every 60 seconds from MFL's transaction feed. A freshness indicator in the toolbar tells you exactly how current your view is — click it to force a refresh if you're feeling impatient. The view is available to all league members, not just the commissioner, because everyone deserves to know they're about to get outbid.
imageAlt: Free Agent page showing the Auction view with live bid amounts, countdown timers, and Place Bid buttons

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Mar 13-14)
summary: 3 bug fixes and style improvements across trade builder, league summary.
description: This week's 3 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:

Trade Builder — Trade submissions now work for all league owners, not just the commissioner — the MFL authentication cookie was being silently stripped during cross-origin redirects, causing every trade proposal to fail. Trade Builder page and My Trades panel now use the site-wide editorial design system — consistent section titles, design tokens, typography scale, and focus states across all trade builder components.

League Summary — League Summary page now uses the site-wide design token system — consistent colors, typography, spacing, and improved accessibility with keyboard-navigable sorting and screen reader announcements.

title: Salary Analytics Goes Editorial
summary: The position salary breakdown page traded its spreadsheet energy for proper editorial design — metric cards, detail rows, and tables that actually look like they belong here.
description: The Salary Analytics page used to have the visual sophistication of a mid-2000s fantasy football message board. Six cards with numbers, some inline styles that fought each other, and a layout that treated mobile users like an afterthought. It worked, technically, in the same way that duct-taping your bumper works — functional, but you wouldn't invite anyone to look at it.

Now it's built on the editorial design system. Each position gets a proper card with a 3-column metrics grid showing franchise tag, extension, and team option thresholds at a glance. Detail rows break out starter averages and medians with the uppercase-label-plus-value pattern. The Top Earners tables use the standard editorial header style — rounded corners, gray-50 backgrounds, proper spacing between the header and the first row. Everything scales cleanly on mobile thanks to container query units, so those seven-figure salary numbers don't overflow their cards on a phone screen.

It's also fully accessible now — ARIA live regions announce data changes when you switch seasons or weeks, the tables have proper column headers, and keyboard navigation works throughout. The Export to Excel button and archive link are still right where you'd expect them. Same data, same functionality, dramatically better presentation.
imageAlt: Salary Analytics page showing editorial-styled position cards with metric grids, detail rows, and top earner tables

title: Salary History Gets the Editorial Treatment
summary: The historical salary trends chart went from bare-bones to best-in-class — tabbed views, accessible data tables, and a design that actually belongs on this site.
description: The salary history chart used to look like it wandered in from a different website. A basic Chart.js line graph with default styling, a legend that did the bare minimum, and zero personality. It got the job done the way a gas station sandwich gets the job done — technically, but you wouldn't brag about it.

Now it's a proper editorial feature. A segmented control lets you toggle between Franchise Tag, Extension, and Team Option trends with smooth data transitions. The custom legend and tooltip follow the site's design language — muted steel-and-sienna palette, uppercase micro labels, tabular numbers that actually align. Every chart type has a collapsible data table underneath for accessibility and for the spreadsheet people who want the raw numbers. The whole thing is keyboard-navigable, screen-reader-friendly, and responsive down to mobile without losing readability.

It's also the first chart in the editorial design system, which means every future chart starts from this foundation. The palette tokens, the tooltip pattern, the legend style — all reusable. Think of it less as a page redesign and more as the chart department finally opening for business.
imageAlt: Salary history page showing a styled line chart with Franchise Tag trends, segmented control tabs, custom legend, and collapsible data table

title: Auction Command Center
summary: The homepage now transforms into an auction war room during free agent season — every link you need, one click away.
description: During auction season, you used to open the homepage, stare at whatever feature announcement happened to be there, and then manually navigate to MFL to place a bid, check results, or see who's left. It had all the workflow efficiency of driving to the airport and then remembering you left your passport at home. Every. Single. Time.

Now, the moment free agent auction season opens, the homepage automatically becomes an Auction Command Center. Place Auction Bid gets the big green button treatment front and center. Auction Summary, Auction Details, Completed Auctions — all the MFL tools are one click away. Plus quick links to Free Agents, Trade Builder, Rosters, Historical Salary Trends, and the Calendar so you can plan your next move without tab-surfing. Quick Rules are right there too, because nobody remembers the bid timer rules until they've already lost an auction.

After the initial 30-day hero window, the homepage goes back to normal but keeps a compact auction strip with your most-used links — because auction season runs through August and you'll still need those shortcuts in July. The same system kicks in for the Rookie Draft too, swapping in draft-specific links and a purple theme when draft day rolls around. Think of it as the homepage finally paying attention to what time of year it is.
imageAlt: Homepage showing the Auction Command Center with Place Auction Bid button, quick rules, and eight quick links for auction tools

title: Submit Trades from the Trade Builder
summary: Build a trade, review the details, and send the proposal — all without leaving theleague.us.
description: The trade builder used to be a very fancy calculator. You could model trades, compare cap impact, and generally feel very smart about your roster decisions — but when it came time to actually pull the trigger, you still had to open MFL in another tab, navigate their labyrinthine trade interface, and manually re-enter everything you'd just spent ten minutes perfecting. It had all the workflow efficiency of writing a grocery list and then leaving it on the kitchen counter.

Now you can submit trade proposals directly from the trade builder. Click 'Submit Trade' and you'll get a confirmation modal that shows both sides of the deal — players, draft picks, cap impact — so you can take one last look before committing to whatever questionable decision you're about to make. Add an optional message (diplomacy points matter), hit send, and the proposal goes straight to MFL. The other owner gets it instantly.

But wait, there's more. The new 'My Trades' panel lets you manage all your pending proposals without ever leaving the page. Accept, reject, withdraw, or counter-offer — all with inline confirmation so you don't accidentally accept a trade that sends your franchise quarterback for a conditional 2028 seventh-rounder. Counter-offers load the original trade right back into the builder so you can adjust and re-submit. It's the full trade lifecycle, from 'I have an idea' to 'I regret this already.'
imageAlt: Trade builder showing the trade confirmation modal with both sides of a proposed trade, cap impact metrics, and a submit button

title: Login Page Redesign
summary: The sign-in page got the editorial treatment — clean labels, proper spacing, and a form that finally looks like it belongs on this site.
description: The old login page had all the visual personality of a hospital check-in kiosk. Two input fields, a button, and the quiet implication that design was somebody else's problem. It worked, technically, the same way a folding chair works at a dinner party — functional, sure, but nobody's impressed.

The new sign-in page follows the same editorial design language as the rest of the site: uppercase micro-labels, considered spacing, a CTA button that actually looks like it wants to be clicked, and a security note that gently reminds you we never store your password. The form is also built as a reusable component now — meaning it can drop into a modal on any page without a single line of duplicate code. Accessibility got the full treatment too: proper focus rings, ARIA error announcements, and keyboard navigation that doesn't fight you.
It's a login page. It shouldn't need a press release. But it also shouldn't look like it was built during a lunch break in 2009.
imageAlt: Redesigned login page with editorial-style uppercase labels, clean input fields, and a prominent Sign In button following the site's design language

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Mar 2-2)
summary: 2 bug fixes and style improvements across free agents.
description: This week's 2 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:

Free Agents — Free Agents page now uses the site's editorial design language: table headers match the clean gray-50 style used throughout, section labels have the signature left-border accent, filter panel labels are consistently styled, and the Clear Filters button no longer looks like it's about to revoke your fantasy license. Free Agents hero section redesigned with a broadcast-style diagonal photo cut inspired by ESPN and FOX Sports graphics — rotating NFL player photos now fill a parallelogram with accent stripes along both edges instead of sitting in a small card.
imageAlt: Free Agents page showing broadcast-style diagonal player photo, editorial table headers, and position filter tabs

title: Contract Declarations
summary: Contract management is built into your roster now. Declare years on new acquisitions, override rookie deals, exercise team options, and extend rookies — no message board required.
description: For nineteen years, contract changes in this league have followed the same ritual: post to the message board, tag the commissioner, wait somewhere between two hours and two months, then check back to see if anything actually happened. It had all the efficiency of faxing a lunch order. Missed a deadline because you forgot your message board password and the commish was at his kid's soccer game? Tough. Forgot to declare years on a waiver pickup because you were busy living your life? That's a one-year contract now, and nobody's losing sleep over it except you.

Contract declarations are now built directly into the roster page. Every contract action — from declaring years on a new acquisition to exercising a first-round team option — lives right where you already manage your team. No message board posts. No waiting. Deadlines are tracked automatically, countdowns tick in real time, and the system knows exactly which players are eligible for what. The goal is near-real-time contract updates: you make the call, the league reflects it.

A note on the beta: This is live, but it's running with training wheels. Every declaration you submit goes through a commissioner approval step before it hits the books. This is intentional — we're validating the process, testing the edge cases, and making sure nobody accidentally signs DeAndre Hopkins to a five-year deal when they meant to click three. Once we're confident everything is airtight, the approval step goes away and deadlines enforce themselves. In the meantime, double-check your submissions on the contract declarations page. Trust, but verify.

description.alt: Roster page in GM mode with demo players showing contract year chips, salary projections, TO badges, and deadline countdowns across the Years and salary columns
description.caption: Demo mode adds four sandbox players to your roster — each one representing a different declaration type.

description: The Tutorial: How It Works — There's a "How It Works" button on the right edge of the roster page that walks you through everything in four steps. It's a guided tutorial with demo players, real modals, and zero consequences. Nothing you click in the demo actually submits. Think of it as voluntary OTAs for contract management.

description.alt: Tutorial Step 1 showing four contract declaration types: New Acquisition, Rookie Override, Team Option, and Rookie Extension, each with their default terms, options, and deadline windows
description.caption: Step 1 breaks down the four declaration types — defaults, options, and deadlines at a glance.

description: New Acquisition — Every player added via waiver bid or free agent signing defaults to a 1-year contract. You can declare 2 to 5 years within a 24-hour window during the season or 48 hours in the offseason. The blue year chip on your roster shows the current contract length and a live countdown to your deadline. Click it, pick your years, and you're done. Miss the window and the default sticks.

description.alt: Contract declaration modal for DeAndre Hopkins showing a New Acquisition type, $500K salary, 1-year current contract, deadline countdown at 17 hours, and year selection buttons for 2 through 5 years
description.caption: Hopkins as a new acquisition — $500K salary, 17 hours to declare, options from 2 to 5 years.

description: Rookie Override — All drafted rookies start on a 4-year Rookie Contract (RC). If you'd rather commit to fewer years — maybe you're not sold on that third-round tight end lasting four seasons — you can override the default down to 1, 2, or 3 years. The deadline is the third Sunday in August, which gives you most of the offseason to decide. The blue chip shows the 4-year default with a day countdown to the cutoff.

description.alt: Contract declaration modal for Fernando Mendoza showing a Rookie Override type, $3.25M salary, 4-year RC contract, 162-day deadline, and year selection buttons for 1 through 3 years
description.caption: Mendoza's rookie override — reduce from the 4-year default to 1, 2, or 3 years before August.

description: Team Option — First-round picks carry a TO (Team Option) badge, which means before Year 4, you have a decision to make. The action button next to the player gives you three paths: exercise the 5th-year team option at the top-10 positional salary average, extend the rookie deal by two years using the extension formula, or do nothing and let the contract play out. This is the NFL-style decision point that separates dynasty thinkers from dynasty hopers.

description.alt: Contract action modal for Hailmary, Hank showing Step 1 of the team option flow with four choices: Team Option at top 10 position average, Rookie Extension for 2 additional years, Cut Player, or Trade Player
description.caption: Hailmary's team option — exercise the 5th year, extend the rookie deal, cut, or trade.

description: Rookie Extension — Drafted rookies on RC contracts who haven't hit Year 4 yet are eligible for a 2-year extension. The salary formula mirrors the veteran extension calculation: it factors in the top-5 positional salary average and spreads the value across the extended years. The action button walks you through the math — extension value per year, the new combined salary, and a year-by-year projection with 10% annual escalation baked in.

description.alt: Contract action modal for Misack, Buck showing the rookie extension flow with salary formula breakdown and year selection
description.caption: Misack's rookie extension — the formula, the projection, and the decision, all in one modal.

description: Reading the Board — You don't need to memorize any of this. The roster page marks every eligible player automatically. Blue year chips mean a declaration is available — click to open. TO badges on first-round picks signal the team option decision. Three-dot action buttons appear next to players with multiple paths (extend, option, cut, trade). Deadline badges count down in real time, shifting from days to hours to a red urgent state as time runs out. If a player is eligible for something, you'll see it.

description.alt: Tutorial Step 3 showing four mock roster rows demonstrating how eligible players are marked with blue year chips, TO badges, action buttons, and deadline countdowns
description.caption: The tutorial's 'Reading the Board' step — every visual cue explained with real examples.

description: What Happens After You Submit — Your declaration lands in the commissioner's review queue. During this beta period, every submission gets a manual once-over before it's applied to MFL. You'll see a pending status on your roster, and once approved, the contract updates automatically — salary adjustments, year changes, designation updates, all synced. Rejected declarations get flagged with a reason so you can resubmit if needed.

description.alt: Commissioner contract declarations page showing the pending review queue, status counters for pending, approved, rejected, and applied declarations, and the recent decisions history
description.caption: The commissioner's view — pending review queue, status dashboard, and decision history.

description: This replaces the message board for contract changes. No more "hey commish, can you update Hopkins to 3 years" posts that get buried under trade chatter and trash talk. Open your roster, click the chip, make the declaration. The demo is there if you want to practice first — four players, four scenarios, zero commitments. And if something looks off after you submit, the declarations page shows every submission, its status, and whether it's been applied. Use it. That's what it's there for.
imageAlt: Contract declaration modal for DeAndre Hopkins showing salary, deadline countdown, and year selection options over the roster page

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Feb 24-Mar 1)
summary: 6 bug fixes and style improvements across navigation & routing, what's new, rosters.
description: This week's 6 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:
Navigation & Routing — Drawer links for Trade Builder, Import Rankings, League Calendar, and League Summary now open reliably on theleague.us again..
What's New — Category badges on What's New cards with thumbnails are now vertically centered on the border at all screen sizes.

Rosters — Opponent average and rank columns in the player modal weekly stats now show real data from MFL instead of placeholder values. Draft pick numbers on the League Planner now show the correct positions from MFL instead of approximations from standings. Extension candidates and franchise tag options on the League Planner now show only your team's players instead of candidates from every team in the league. Rookie and draft prospect headshots now show their college photo instead of the generic placeholder across Free Agents, Rosters, Draft Predictor, and Trade Builder.
imageAlt: Fernando Mendoza's player details modal showing his Indiana college headshot loaded via the new college headshot fallback

title: Free Agents, From Scratch
summary: The Free Agents page got rebuilt from the ground up — hero banner, college logos, advanced filters, and enough sorting options to make a spreadsheet jealous.
description: The old Free Agents page was functional the way a phone book is functional — technically all the information was there, but finding what you wanted required patience, squinting, and a vague sense that there had to be a better way. Sorting was limited, filtering was nonexistent, and if you wanted to compare a player's snap count to their draft pedigree you were toggling between tabs like it was 2011.

The rebuilt page starts with a rotating hero banner featuring players from the available pool, because even a research tool deserves a little curb appeal. Below that, position pills give you instant counts — 86 QBs, 132 RBs, 211 WRs — and a search bar handles the 'I know who I want' moments. The new Filters panel lets you slice by NFL team, age range, height, weight, experience, and draft round, all collapsible so it stays out of your way when you don't need it. The Stats view packs in everything from college logos to snap percentages, and the Rankings view syncs with your imported rankings so you can sort by your own board. Every column is sortable, every row opens the redesigned player modal, and the whole thing remembers your last view preference.

Think of it less as a free agent list and more as a scouting department that fits in a browser tab. Use it before the auction to find the undervalued 26-year-old WR2 nobody's talking about. Or use it after to confirm that yes, you did in fact overpay for a 30-year-old running back. The data doesn't lie — it just waits patiently for you to ask the right question.
imageAlt: Redesigned Free Agents page with broadcast-style diagonal photo hero, editorial position filters, and sortable stats table showing top available players

title: Player Modal, Reimagined
summary: The player details modal got the editorial treatment — less scrolling, more answers, and a layout that actually respects your time.
description: The old player details modal was the informational equivalent of a junk drawer. Six sections, equal visual weight, the data you actually cared about — weekly results — buried below height, weight, and birthdate like some kind of NFL combine scouting report from 2004. Want to know if a free agent is worth picking up? That'll be four scrolls and a squint, please.

The redesigned modal flips the hierarchy entirely. At the top: a clean hero section with the player's headshot, name, team, and age — everything you need to confirm you clicked the right guy. Below that, three metric cards give you the instant 'worth it' signal: total fantasy points, points per game, and contract status. Then the weekly results table — promoted from the basement to prime real estate — with color-coded matchup difficulty, compact rows for unplayed weeks, and your fantasy manager's face in the status column. Supporting details like height, weight, draft pedigree, and contract breakdown are still there, just tucked into a compact section at the bottom where they belong.

The whole thing works on mobile too — slides up as a bottom sheet, hides the less-useful columns, and keeps the information you actually open a modal to see front and center. Click any player name on the Free Agents page and see for yourself. Or don't, and keep scrolling past someone's college to find out if they scored points last week.
imageAlt: Redesigned player details modal showing Joe Burrow's profile with hero section, metric cards, and weekly results table

title: The Dead Money Awards
summary: Finally, a page dedicated to celebrating the league's worst financial decisions. You know who you are.
description: For years, the GM MVP Awards have been handing out trophies to the shrewd, the lucky, and the suspiciously well-informed. Meanwhile, the rest of you have been quietly lighting salary cap on fire and hoping nobody noticed. Well, someone noticed. The Dead Money Awards are here to immortalize every overpay, every busted contract, and every player who collected a paycheck and contributed exactly nothing in return.

The Jerry Jones Award goes to the GM whose best possible starting lineup still managed to be the worst value in the league — because spending $32 million to score fewer points than a team spending half that is a special kind of talent. The Brock Osweiler Award honors the individual player who burned the most salary per point scored, named after the QB who was so bad the Texans literally paid the Browns a second-round pick to take his contract away. And then there's the Zero-Point Hall of Shame: rostered players with real salary who scored exactly zero points. Not one. Not a half-point garbage time catch. Zero.

Every season is covered, every position has its worst, and the year filter lets you relive the financial mismanagement of seasons past. Think of it as a public service — or at least a cautionary tale for auction day. Check who's on the list before you bid. Or don't, and see yourself here next year.
imageAlt: Dead Money Awards page showing the league's worst contracts and overpays by season

title: The League Has an App Now
summary: That's right. A nineteen-year-old fantasy football league has its own app. Install it to your home screen and try to act surprised.
description: Let's address the elephant in the room: yes, a sixteen-team dynasty fantasy football league that has existed since 2007 now has its own dedicated app. Not a shared app. Not a link to MFL's mobile site that looks like it was designed for a Blackberry. An actual, installable, icon-on-your-home-screen app, sitting right there between Instagram and whatever meditation app you downloaded in January and opened once. The audacity is not lost on us.

Now, before you start drafting your acceptance speech for the App Store — it's not in the App Store. It's a Progressive Web App, which means you install it straight from your browser. On iPhone, hit the share button and tap "Add to Home Screen." On Android, tap the three-dot menu. On desktop, look for the install icon in Chrome's address bar. No downloads, no updates, no 47 permission requests asking for access to your contacts, camera, and existential dread. It just works.

Here's what the app does right now: it logs you in, remembers who you are for 90 days, defaults every roster page to your team, and highlights your franchise across the site. That's it. That's the app. We built an entire installable application so you could stop scrolling through a dropdown to find your own team. In the future, this is where lineup submissions, free agent bidding, and other MFL actions will live — but for now, it's a very fancy, very persistent bookmark that knows your name. Install it anyway. You know you want to.
imageAlt: The League installed as a Progressive Web App on a mobile home screen

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Feb 17-23)
summary: 20 bug fixes and style improvements across navigation & routing, rosters, rankings, design system & theming, and more.
description: This week's 20 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:

Navigation & Routing — URL rewriting now uses Astro middleware instead of Vercel rewrites for more reliable page routing. The /theleague path prefix is now hidden on theleague.us so URLs are cleaner (e.g., theleague.us/rosters instead of theleague.us/theleague/rosters). Mobile and tablet header now stacks nav icons below the logo for a tidier layout.
Rosters — Roster page now shows a random team for new visitors instead of always defaulting to the same team.

Rankings — Removed extra right padding from composite ranking columns for tighter alignment. Ranking column border styles refined for better visual separation between sources. Player matching for imported rankings now handles nicknames (e.g., Mike/Michael) for more accurate matches.

Design System & Theming — Ranking type badge colors softened while maintaining WCAG AA contrast. New --table-header-gradient design token unifies all table header styles across the site. Hardcoded fonts and icon sizes replaced with design tokens for consistency. Accent link hover color now uses brand green instead of a non-brand amber.
Import Rankings — Ranking type badges on import page now use solid colors for better readability. Import rankings cards now have white backgrounds instead of gray.
League Summary — League summary subtitle spacing tightened from 5rem to 2rem.

Free Agents — Free agent average rank column colors updated for better contrast. Average ranking column border made symmetrical on the Free Agents page. Defense player avatars now properly centered using flex alignment. Defense avatars are now circular with smaller, better-proportioned logos. Fixed empty free agents list caused by missing projection data by switching to projectedScores.
Homepage — Homepage card hover color corrected to use the green design token.
imageAlt: Free Agents page showing defense player avatars, ranking color badges, and table header gradient styling

title: League Planner
summary: Your roster's Next Year tab now reshuffles itself based on where you are in the offseason — because what matters in January is not what matters in March.
description: The offseason isn't one season — it's four seasons wearing the same trench coat. Tagging window priorities look nothing like free agency priorities, which look nothing like draft night priorities. The old Next Year tab didn't care about any of that. It showed you the same static layout whether you were evaluating franchise tag candidates in January or scrambling for draft picks in April. The League Planner fixes this by rearranging itself around whatever phase of the offseason you're actually in.

After Week 10, extension and franchise tag tools rise to the top of the page because that's what matters before the tagging deadline. Once free agency opens, a Potential Targets card takes the lead — complete with player headshots, positions, and your imported ranking columns — surfacing the free agents your roster actually needs. Missing a top-8 quarterback or tight end? Only have one top-16 wide receiver? The Planner flags the gaps and recommends targets. During the draft, your 2026 and 2027 pick assets take center stage so you can plan trades without flipping between tabs.

It's the difference between a to-do list and a to-do list that knows what day it is. Open the Planner in February and it's thinking about tags. Open it in March and it's thinking about free agents. Open it in April and it's thinking about the draft. You should probably be thinking about those things too.
imageAlt: League Planner showing offseason phase-aware roster tools with free agent targets and draft picks

title: Extension Calculator Fix
summary: Veteran extension and franchise tag calculations now correctly use the previous season's frozen salary averages.
description: For a brief window after the February league year rollover, the extension and franchise tag calculators were doing their math with all the reliability of a rookie quarterback in a snow game. The problem? They were pulling salary averages from the new league year's roster pool — a pool where half the teams hadn't even finished assembling their rosters yet. The result was position averages that looked like they were generated by a random number generator rather than actual contract data.

The fix locks all extension and franchise tag calculations onto the previous season's week 14 frozen salary snapshot — the gold standard of salary data, captured when rosters are at their most complete and stable. This frozen data stays in play until the current season reaches its own week 14 freeze, ensuring you never get calculations based on incomplete information. If you used the Trade Builder, Rosters, or Contract Tools pages during the rollover window and something looked off, this is why — and it's now corrected.

title: My Rank Composite
summary: Pick your trusted ranking sources, assign weights, and see a single personalized "My Rank" column on the Free Agents page.
description: The arms race of ranking sources has officially reached the point where owners need a ranking of their rankings. With seven import sources available — and some owners hoarding all seven — the Free Agents page was turning into a spreadsheet that would make an accountant weep. Something had to give, and that something is the new My Rank composite.

Head to the Import Rankings page, check the boxes next to the sources you actually trust, and assign each a weight: 1x for sources you respect, 2x for sources you swear by, and 3x for whatever gospel you've decided is the absolute truth this offseason. Once you've selected at least two sources, a blended "My Rank" column appears as the very first ranking column on the Free Agents page. Your composite members are grouped together with a visual separator so you can still see how the individual rankings compare to your custom blend.

It's worth noting that this is a weighted average, not a popularity contest. If you give KeepTradeCut 3x weight and everything else 1x, KTC's rankings will dominate your composite. Adjust accordingly — or don't, and blame the algorithm when your auction bids don't work out.
imageAlt: Import Rankings page showing ranking source selection with weight controls for building a My Rank composite

title: What's New Blog Redesign
summary: Browse updates with category filters, full detail pages, and a timeline sidebar.
description: The What's New page has received the kind of makeover usually reserved for a franchise quarterback getting a new helmet design — except this one is actually functional. The original flat list of updates was fine when there were five entries, but with the feature count now pushing thirty, it had all the navigability of a phone book.

The redesign introduces category filter tabs so you can zero in on New Pages, New Features, or Enhancements without scrolling past bug fixes you don't care about. Every entry now has its own dedicated detail page with a full description, category badge, and prev/next navigation for the chronological browsers among us. On desktop, a sticky timeline sidebar with color-coded dots lets you jump to any month's updates instantly.

Entries you haven't seen since your last visit are marked with a NEW badge, which is either a helpful convenience or a guilt trip depending on how often you check in. The system tracks your last visit via cookie, so clearing your browser data means everything looks new again — a fresh start, if you need one.
imageAlt: What's New page with category filter tabs, featured entry cards, and timeline sidebar

title: Salary Archive
summary: Browse and download every historical salary spreadsheet from 2009 to 2025.
description: For seventeen years, the league's salary data lived in a scattered archipelago of Google Sheets, each one lovingly maintained by hand and shared via group chat links that expired approximately thirty seconds after being posted. Finding the 2016 salary averages required the kind of archaeological dig usually reserved for ancient civilizations.

The Salary Archive page puts every historical Tagged Salary Averages spreadsheet from 2009 to 2025 in one place, each year linking directly to the original file. It's the complete record — from the league's earliest days of manual spreadsheet management through the transition to automated MFL API data. The Salary Analytics page also now includes an Export to Excel button that generates a multi-sheet workbook with one tab per position plus a Position Summary overview, so you can take current data with you.

Whether you're settling a debate about what quarterbacks cost in 2014 or building a historical model for your auction strategy, the data is now exactly one click away instead of buried in someone's Google Drive.
imageAlt: Salary Archive page listing historical salary spreadsheets from 2009 to 2025

title: Ranking Source in Roster Column
summary: The roster rank column now displays which ranking source is being used instead of a generic label.
description: A small change with outsized quality-of-life impact: the rank column on the Roster page now actually tells you which ranking source it's displaying. Previously, the column header just said "Rank" — leaving you to either remember which source you'd selected, squint at the numbers and try to reverse-engineer it, or simply accept the mystery rankings on faith.

The header now shows the source abbreviation directly — Sleep, FBG, KTC, CBS, and so on — with the full source name revealed on hover. It's the kind of thing you didn't know you needed until you found yourself staring at a rank column wondering if those were dynasty ranks or redraft ranks and whether you should be panicking about your WR3 or not.
imageAlt: Roster page showing ranking source abbreviation in the column header

title: Salary Analytics
summary: Explore salary benchmarks by position with starter averages, medians, and full player breakdowns.
description: The Salary Benchmarks page has been promoted to Salary Analytics, and the upgrade is roughly equivalent to going from a box score to a full analytics department. Every position now displays starter average and starter median based on realistic league starter counts — not just the overall average that gets dragged down by the deep bench players making league minimum.

Browse the complete ranked salary list for any position with a Show More button to reveal the full depth chart of spending. The year selector lets you compare salary trends across every season back to 2021, and weekly snapshot switching shows how values shifted throughout the year — useful for tracking how that mid-season blockbuster trade reshaped the market.

The addition of the Export to Excel button means you can now download a multi-sheet workbook with one tab per position for whatever offline analysis your inner data scientist demands. For the owners who built their entire draft strategy in Excel — you know who you are — this one's for you.
imageAlt: Salary Analytics page with position benchmarks, starter averages, and player salary breakdowns

title: Import Rankings
summary: Import player rankings from FantasyPros, FootballGuys, Sleeper, and more — then drag to set your preferred display order.
description: In a league where information is currency, the Import Rankings page is essentially a money printer. Seven major fantasy football ranking sites — FantasyPros, FootballGuys, Dynasty League Football, KeepTradeCut, Sleeper, Yahoo, and CBS Sports — can now feed their rankings directly into the league's tools using simple browser bookmarklets.

The process is deliberately low-tech and high-trust: drag a bookmarklet to your browser's bookmarks bar, visit your ranking site of choice, click the bookmarklet, and the data flows in. Once imported, drag and drop your saved rankings to control which columns appear and in what order on the Free Agents page. Up to eight ranking columns display for free agents, or five when rostered players are included.

Every byte of ranking data stays private in your browser's local storage — never sent to the server, never shared with other league members. Your scouting advantage is yours alone, which is exactly how it should be in a league where pre-auction intelligence can be worth millions in cap space.
imageAlt: Import Rankings page with bookmarklet instructions and draggable ranking source list

title: Free Agent Scouting Tool
summary: Research free agents with ADP rankings, snap counts, and detailed player profiles.
description: Auction prep used to involve twelve browser tabs, three spreadsheets, and the quiet resignation that you'd never have all the data in one place. The Free Agent Scouting Tool consolidates the essentials into a single sortable table: ADP Redraft rankings, ADP Dynasty rankings, and NFL snap count data from last season — including total snaps and snap percentage — all side by side.

Filter by position, search by name or NFL team, and toggle rookies in or out depending on whether you're feeling bold or cautious this offseason. Every column is sortable, which means you can find the high-usage sleepers — lots of snaps, low ADP — or confirm your suspicion that the player everyone's hyping barely saw the field last year.

This is the pre-auction research hub the league has needed since the first time someone overpaid for a player whose snap count could fit on a Post-it note. Use it before the auction, or don't — and then use it after the auction to figure out where things went wrong.
imageAlt: Free Agents scouting table with player rankings, snap counts, and position filters

title: What's New Page
summary: Track every new feature and improvement in one place.
description: New features used to be announced in the group chat, where they'd enjoy approximately forty-five seconds of visibility before being buried under an avalanche of trade proposals, injury debates, and whatever meme someone found that morning. The What's New page is the league's official changelog — a permanent, browsable record of every feature and improvement as it ships.

Each entry includes a date, description, and a direct link to the relevant page so you can try new features immediately instead of wondering where they live. It's the difference between hearing about a new restaurant and being handed the address, the menu, and a reservation.
imageAlt: What's New changelog page showing feature entries with dates and category badges

title: GM/Coach Mode Toggle
summary: Switch between GM and Coach views on your roster page.
description: This one started with a simple request from The Dream: he wanted to see the top scores from every player. What he got was an entire second perspective on the roster page. The GM tab was always there — salaries, contracts, escalation projections, trade value — but not everyone opens the roster page thinking about cap space. Some people open it on Sunday morning wondering who to start.

The Coach tab strips away the front office and replaces it with what matters on game day. Player averages, opponent matchups, injury designations, Vegas lines, over/unders, and even stadium weather — everything a coach needs to set a lineup without wading through contract details. GM mode is still one click away for when the trade itch hits, but Coach mode is for the people who just want to win this week.

Coach mode is available to all users, with a demo experience for visitors who want to kick the tires. It's accessible directly via URL so anyone who's already decided which hat they're wearing today can skip straight to the view they need.
imageAlt: Roster page in Coach Mode showing matchup data and start/sit columns

title: Trade Bait Marketplace
summary: See which players teams have listed as available for trade.
description: The trade deadline is approaching, and the eternal question echoes across the league: "Who's available?" Previously, answering that question required individually messaging every owner — a process roughly as efficient as going door-to-door asking if anyone wants to sell their house. The Trade Bait Marketplace eliminates the detective work by surfacing every player that teams have flagged as available for trade, all in one view.

Built directly into the Trade Builder, the marketplace shows trade bait across all sixteen teams, filterable by position. During the February transition period — when the league straddles two league years — trade bait from both the current and incoming seasons is displayed so you're working with the full picture.
See a player you like? You're already in the Trade Builder. Start dragging.
imageAlt: Trade Builder page showing the Trade Bait Marketplace with available players

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Feb 10-16)
summary: 17 bug fixes and style improvements across navigation & routing, calendar, league summary, rosters, and more.
description: This week's 17 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:
Navigation & Routing — The site is now accessible at theleague.us with proper domain routing, forum proxy, and root redirect. GM Central link updated to use the correct www.theleague.us domain.

Calendar — Calendar now uses the correct calendar year with a previous-year option and swapped category colors. NFL Draft and Season Start events now use the NFL logo icon with multi-color SVG support. Rookie draft date corrected to Saturday after NFL Draft week. Auction results link fixed to the correct MFL module. Single-day event deadlines now default to 8:45 PM PT.
League Summary — Year range now correctly shows 5 years starting from the current league year (2026-2030). Quartile indicators switched from background colors to text colors for better readability.

Rosters — Veteran extension table columns consolidated to prevent overflow on smaller screens. Draft picks card no longer goes missing on the Next Year view after league year rollover. Demo mode opponent averages and ranks now vary per player for realistic sorting.
Trade Builder — React integration added for Trade Builder components to resolve rendering issues.
Playoffs — Playoffs description corrected from 6 to 7 teams to match actual league rules.
Homepage — Hero banner CTA button alignment fixed on mobile. Mobile alignment and padding improved across homepage and What's New page.
Other — Build failure when MFL API is unavailable during offseason now handled gracefully. CSS class scoping fixed in icon sprite. Icon gallery names display single-line with hidden scrollbar.
imageAlt: League calendar showing NFL logo icons, corrected dates, and color-coded event categories

title: League Summary Page
summary: Compare all 16 teams across cap, roster, and contract metrics.
description: Trying to compare all sixteen teams used to require the patience of a saint and the memory of an elephant. Open one roster page, jot down some numbers, open another, compare, forget what the first team's cap situation looked like, go back, repeat until your browser has more tabs than your salary cap has dollars. The League Summary page ends this madness with a single dashboard.

Fourteen categories of competitive intelligence — salary cap usage, dead money, contract length distribution, draft capital, roster age, extension candidates, and more — are laid out across all sixteen teams in one sortable, scannable view. Five years of historical data lets you track which teams are building for the future and which ones are mortgaging it.
This is the page you open when someone in the group chat says their team is in great cap shape. Trust, but verify.
imageAlt: League Summary dashboard comparing all 16 teams across salary cap and roster metrics

title: Trade Builder
summary: Simulate trades and instantly see salary cap impact for both teams.
description: This one's for Wabbit. The league's most prolific trader — a true fantasy football day trader — used to evaluate every deal the old-fashioned way: fire up a spreadsheet, manually look up salaries and contract lengths for both sides, calculate escalation percentages by hand, and hope nobody fat-fingered a decimal point. If anyone was going to stress-test a trade tool into existence, it was going to be the owner who treats the trade wire like a full-time job.

Select any two teams, drag players and draft picks between them, and watch the salary cap impact update in real time. The tool automatically calculates multi-year cap projections with the league's 10% annual escalation, because nobody wants to discover three years from now that a deal they loved in February is eating their cap alive. By default, it suggests the two teams with the most cap room — on the theory that cap-rich teams make the most willing trade partners — but you can switch to any matchup in the league.

Mock drafts let you rehearse draft night before it happens. The Trade Builder does the same thing for trades — run as many scenarios as you want, see exactly whether the cap math works, and walk into the negotiation already knowing the answer. No more spreadsheets, no more guessing, no more "I think that works under the cap." Early reports suggest Wabbit's trade volume is up roughly 30% since launch, which is either a ringing endorsement or a warning sign depending on whether you're in his division.
imageAlt: Trade Builder showing two-team player selection with salary cap impact analysis

title: What's Next Timeline
summary: The homepage now shows your current, next, and upcoming league events.
description: The homepage now answers the question that used to require checking the league calendar, the group chat, and occasionally a league member's surprisingly accurate memory: "What's happening next?" A three-card timeline at the top of the page shows the current event, the next upcoming event, and the one after that — each with countdown timers and direct action links.

The cards update automatically as events pass, so the page always reflects the current state of the league calendar. During busy periods like the offseason, where tagging windows, free agency, and the draft can overlap, this becomes the single fastest way to know what you should be paying attention to right now.

It's personalized to your team where applicable, so links point to your specific roster or franchise tools rather than generic league pages. The days of missing a deadline because you forgot to check the calendar are — theoretically — over.
imageAlt: Homepage showing the What's Next timeline with three upcoming league event cards

title: League Calendar
summary: View all important league dates and deadlines in one place.
description: Every league event for the season, in one place, color-coded and countdown-enabled. The League Calendar page displays tagging periods, free agency windows, draft dates, the trading deadline, playoff rounds, and every other date that matters — organized by category with visual differentiation so you can scan for the events relevant to your current planning horizon.

Events include countdown timers that tell you exactly how many days until the next deadline, action links to the relevant league tools, and status badges indicating whether something is active, upcoming, or urgent. A year selector lets you browse the previous and upcoming seasons' calendars for long-term planning.
This is the page you bookmark and check every Monday morning. Or you can keep relying on group chat reminders that inevitably arrive twelve hours after the deadline. Your call.
imageAlt: League Calendar page with color-coded events and countdown timers

title: ESPN Player Headshots
summary: Player cards now show real ESPN headshot photos.
description: Player cards across the site now display actual human faces instead of the featureless silhouette placeholders that made every roster look like a witness protection program. Real ESPN headshot photos have been integrated across all player-facing components, displayed at 150% zoom for a clean, cropped presentation.

It's a cosmetic upgrade, yes, but there's something to be said for being able to visually identify the players you're investing millions of cap dollars in. The system falls back gracefully to MFL photos when ESPN data isn't available, and to a default placeholder as a last resort — so even the most obscure practice squad player gets some kind of representation.
imageAlt: Player cards displaying real ESPN headshot photos instead of placeholder silhouettes

title: Custom Football Icon Set
summary: A complete set of custom-designed football icons throughout the app.
description: The navigation drawer and header have been outfitted with a complete set of custom-designed SVG football icons, replacing the generic icon library that made the app look like it could have been about anything from project management to recipe tracking. The new icons include purpose-built designs for lineups, trades, drafts, standings, salary management, and more.

It's a small touch that adds up to a significantly more cohesive experience. When you click on the trades icon, it looks like it belongs in a football league, not a stock portfolio app. The entire set is built as an SVG sprite for fast loading, which means you get better visuals with less overhead — the rare upgrade that doesn't cost you any cap space.
imageAlt: Navigation drawer showcasing custom-designed SVG football icons for each page

title: Navigation Drawer Redesign
summary: A new collapsible side drawer for faster navigation between pages.
description: The old top navigation bar served the league faithfully for its era, but with the page count growing faster than a rookie's contract demands, something more scalable was in order. The new navigation drawer is a full slide-out panel with grouped sections, league switching, and team information — think of it as the difference between a folding map and a GPS.

On desktop, the drawer pushes page content aside when open and collapses to a slim icon rail when you need more screen space. On mobile, it overlays as a full-screen panel with a dimmed backdrop. Keyboard navigation, focus trapping, and Escape-to-close are all built in for the power users who refuse to touch a mouse.

The drawer footer shows your team's logo, owner name, and franchise info — a small personalization that serves as a constant reminder of which dynasty you're building. Or, depending on your roster, which dynasty you're rebuilding.
imageAlt: Redesigned navigation drawer with grouped sections, league switching, and team info
